🇳🇱 Amsterdam Insider Guide

10 Best Things To Do in Amsterdam

The definitive local guide — 52 attractions across 6 categories, a 3-day itinerary, and skip-the-line tickets. Written by someone who actually lives here.

CITY INSIGHTS

Amsterdam packs world-class museums, stunning architecture, great food, and unique canal culture into a compact, walkable city. The canal ring alone justifies the trip.

Amsterdam started as a 12th-century fishing village on the river Amstel. Today, it is one of the world's most iconic cities, defined by its 165 canals, 1,500 bridges, and a legacy of artistic and commercial supremacy from the Dutch Golden Age. Whether you're here for the masterpieces of Rembrandt and Van Gogh, the haunting history of the Anne Frank House, or simply to cycle along the UNESCO-listed waterways, Amsterdam is a city that rewards both the checklist-tourist and the aimless wanderer.

Essential Info

🌤
Best Time to Visit
April – May & Sept – Oct
📅
Ideal Trip Length
3 – 4 days
💶
Daily Budget
€70 – €150
🌍
UNESCO Heritage
Canal Ring (since 2010)
🚲
Getting Around
Bike, tram, or on foot
🚶
Canal Ring Walk
Stroll the UNESCO-listed Herengracht, Keizersgracht & Prinsengracht — free, all day, all year.
🌿
Vondelpark
47 hectares of parkland, free open-air theatre (June–Aug), and the city's best people-watching.
🔭
NEMO Rooftop
The rooftop terrace of NEMO Science Museum is free to access — one of the best city views.
⛪
Westerkerk
Free to enter Amsterdam's tallest church, near the Anne Frank House. Rembrandt is buried here.
🌸
Bloemenmarkt
The world's only floating flower market is free to browse. On Singel canal near Koningsplein.
⛴
Free Ferry to Noord
The GVB ferries behind Centraal Station to Amsterdam Noord run 24/7 and are completely free.
DISTRICTS

Neighbourhoods

Jordaan

Brown cafés · 9 Straatjes · weekend markets

Jordaan

Brown cafés · 9 Straatjes · weekend markets

The Jordaan started as a mistake. When Amsterdam expanded its canal ring in the early 17th century, the city planners drew the new residential quarter to the west — narrow streets, dense housing, no grand waterways. It was built for workers: tanners, cloth-dyers, and craftsmen who couldn't afford the canal ring. Today the Jordaan has some of the highest residential property prices in the Netherlands, yet it has absorbed its own gentrification without quite losing its character.

Explore District

De Pijp

Albert Cuyp market · craft beer · best food in the city

De Pijp

Albert Cuyp market · craft beer · best food in the city

De Pijp was built fast and cheap in the late 19th century to house Amsterdam's growing working class. The streets were laid out in a rigid grid — long, narrow blocks that gave the neighbourhood its name: 'the pipe.' What makes De Pijp unusual is the specific sequence of communities that settled there — Surinamese, Moroccan, Turkish, and later, art students.

Explore District

Amsterdam Noord

Amsterdam Noord

Amsterdam Noord was, for most of its history, the city Amsterdam pretended didn't exist. The IJ waterway kept it separate. Today the NDSM wharf is Amsterdam's most concentrated creative district — home to Greenpeace, MTV, and significant street art.

Explore District

Museumplein

Rijksmuseum · Van Gogh · Stedelijk — three museums, one lawn

Museumplein

Rijksmuseum · Van Gogh · Stedelijk — three museums, one lawn

In 1877, Amsterdam set aside a large field for culture. The result — three world-class institutions sharing a single square kilometre of lawn — is unique in Europe. On a good day, you can walk from Rembrandt's Night Watch to Van Gogh's Sunflowers in fifteen minutes.

Explore District
PLAN

Featured Collections

9 museums

Museums & Art

Rijksmuseum, Van Gogh, Stedelijk, Moco, Rembrandthuis — three world-class museums you can walk between on one afternoon, on a single square kilometre of lawn.

Explore Museums
8 sites

History & Heritage

Anne Frank House, the Royal Palace, Begijnhof, and hidden 17th-century churches in plain sight. Amsterdam's history is darker and stranger than you expect.

Explore History
8 cruise options

Canal Cruises & Water

Amsterdam has more canals than Venice. Open boat, classic glass-top, evening cruise, or the pancake boat — you need to be on the water at least once.

See All Cruises
9 options

Nature, Flowers & Day Trips

Keukenhof's 7 million tulips, Vondelpark, Artis Zoo, and half-day trips to Delft, Rotterdam, and a medieval castle 20 minutes from the city centre.

Plan a Day Out
9 experiences

Experiences & Entertainment

Swing 100m above the city, taste a century of Heineken, or step inside an immersive light show. The non-museum bucket list — for first-timers and repeat visitors alike.

See All Experiences
9 tours & walks

Neighbourhoods & Tours

The Jordaan, De Pijp, Amsterdam Noord, and the canal ring Centrum — each neighbourhood is a different city. Guided walks, bike tours, and self-guided city games.

Browse Tours
ANSWERS

Common Questions

What is Amsterdam best known for? ▼
Amsterdam is best known for its canal network (a UNESCO World Heritage Site), world-class museums like the Rijksmuseum and Anne Frank House, vibrant cycling culture, and the historic Jordaan neighbourhood.
How many days do you need in Amsterdam? ▼
3 days is the sweet spot for a first visit — enough to cover the top museums, canal districts, and neighbourhoods without feeling rushed. A long weekend (4 days) lets you explore further afield.
What is the best time to visit Amsterdam? ▼
April–May for tulip season and mild weather, or September–October for fewer crowds and golden-hour canal light. 2026 is especially exciting with WorldPride (July 25 – Aug 8).
Do I need to pre-book Amsterdam attractions? ▼
Yes, for the major ones. Anne Frank House sells out weeks in advance and does not accept walk-ins. The Van Gogh Museum requires online booking. Always book at least 1–2 weeks ahead in summer.

the city?
